title: Resource Management: Water
examTip: For water management, know one large-scale scheme (China's South-North Transfer or desalination in Saudi Arabia) and one small-scale sustainable scheme (WaterAid boreholes or Rajasthan johads) — and evaluate each.
introTips: Large-scale: China South-North Water Transfer (cost $78bn, displaced 300,000) OR Saudi desalination.
Small-scale: WaterAid boreholes (~£5,000, serves 500 people) OR Rajasthan johads revival.
The Aral Sea is the most powerful example of what overuse of water can cause — know it.
intro: You use water every day without thinking about it — but for 2 billion people, getting access to safe water is a daily struggle. Water is distributed incredibly unevenly around the planet: Canada has more freshwater than it will ever need while Kuwait depends almost entirely on desalination. And we're using it faster than it replenishes. Understanding why water is where it is, why some people go without, and how we can manage it better — that's what this lesson is about.

keyPoints.heading: Water distribution and demand
keyPoints.content: Water is distributed very unevenly. The Amazon basin receives around 2,500mm of rainfall per year; the Sahara less than 25mm. Demand for water is rising due to population growth, economic development (industry uses large amounts of water), and agricultural expansion (agriculture accounts for 70% of global freshwater use; industry 20%; domestic just 10%). Per capita water use varies enormously: USA approximately 2,200 litres per person per day; UK approximately 140 litres; Mozambique approximately 10 litres. Water-surplus countries include Canada, Brazil, Norway and Iceland — all have high rainfall relative to population. Water-scarce countries include Kuwait, UAE, Libya and Egypt — Egypt depends on the Nile for 97% of its water, making it almost entirely dependent on a single river that flows through other sovereign nations.
writeBullets: Amazon ~2,500mm/year rainfall; Sahara <25mm — extreme unevenness
Agriculture = 70% of global freshwater use; industry 20%; domestic 10%
Per capita use: USA 2,200L/day; UK 140L; Mozambique 10L
Water surplus — Canada, Brazil, Norway (high rainfall, lower population pressure)
Water scarce — Kuwait, UAE, Libya; Egypt 97% dependent on the Nile
Demand rising — population growth, economic development, agricultural expansion
keyPoints.memTip: Agriculture is the big thirst — 70% of all freshwater goes to farming. Domestic use is just 10%.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The Egypt/Nile dependency figure (97%) is striking and very exam-worthy — it shows how geopolitically vulnerable water security can be.

keyPoints.heading: Causes of water insecurity
keyPoints.content: Water insecurity has both physical and human causes. Physical: low or unreliable rainfall (sub-Saharan Africa, Middle East — some years get almost nothing); high temperatures cause high evaporation rates (North Africa, Australia), reducing effective water availability; seasonal variation (monsoon regions have months of flood followed by months of drought). Human causes: pollution — industrial waste (the Ganges in India receives factory effluent, untreated sewage and religious waste); agricultural runoff (nitrate and phosphate fertilisers causing algal blooms that kill aquatic life); sewage (1 billion people still defecate in the open). Overuse: the Aral Sea was once the world's 4th largest lake — Soviet irrigation schemes diverted the rivers feeding it for cotton farming; it lost 90% of its volume, exposing toxic salt flats and collapsing the fishing industry. Poor infrastructure: the UK loses approximately 25% of water to leakage; many LICs lose 50% or more. Climate change is altering rainfall patterns and increasing drought frequency.
writeBullets: Low/unreliable rainfall — sub-Saharan Africa, Middle East; drought is recurring
High evaporation — North Africa, Australia; reduces accessible water
Seasonal variation — monsoon floods then months of drought
Pollution — Ganges (industrial waste, sewage); agricultural runoff (nitrates, algal blooms)
Overuse — Aral Sea: 4th largest lake, lost 90% volume from Soviet cotton irrigation
Poor infrastructure — UK loses 25% to leakage; LICs lose 50%+
Climate change — shifting rainfall patterns, more frequent and severe droughts
keyPoints.memTip: RLPOP-C — Rainfall low, Low evaporation (err, high), Pollution, Overuse, Poor infrastructure, Climate change.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The Aral Sea is one of geography's most powerful examples — it shows what can happen when water is overused at scale. Always describe the scale of the disaster.

keyPoints.heading: Large-scale water management
keyPoints.content: Large-scale water management schemes attempt to bring water where it is needed in large quantities. China's South-North Water Transfer Project is the world's largest water transfer scheme: three canal systems stretching 1,200km transfer 45 billion m³ of water per year from the wet south to the arid north; cost approximately $78 billion; controversially displaced around 300,000 people. Desalination removes salt from seawater or brackish water — Saudi Arabia gets over 70% of its drinking water from 33 desalination plants; Israel uses desalination for approximately 80% of domestic water. Desalination is very energy-intensive (expensive, high carbon footprint) and produces concentrated brine that must be disposed of (harms marine environments when discharged). Dams and reservoirs: Three Gorges Dam (China — world's largest; 22.5 GW HEP; displaced 1.3 million people); Aswan High Dam (Egypt — Nile water storage and hydroelectric power). Groundwater extraction: drilling boreholes into aquifers — the Ogallala Aquifer (USA) and fossil water under the Sahara are being depleted faster than they recharge, creating a long-term supply problem.
writeBullets: China South-North Water Transfer — 1,200km canals; 45bn m³/year; $78bn; displaced 300,000
Desalination — removes salt from seawater; Saudi Arabia 70%+ drinking water from 33 plants
Desalination drawbacks — very energy-intensive; expensive; brine harms marine environments
Three Gorges Dam — world's largest; 22.5 GW; displaced 1.3m people
Aswan High Dam — Nile water storage for Egypt; HEP generation
Groundwater extraction — Ogallala Aquifer (USA) being depleted faster than recharge
keyPoints.memTip: Transfer, Desalinate, Dam, Drill — four large-scale water supply options, all with trade-offs.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Know the China South-North Transfer Project figures — they're specific and impressive. 1,200km, $78bn, 300,000 displaced.

keyPoints.heading: Sustainable water management
keyPoints.content: Sustainable water management aims to meet current needs without depleting supplies for future generations. WaterAid is a prominent UK NGO that sinks boreholes with hand pumps in rural Africa and South Asia, training local technicians to maintain them. A single borehole costs approximately £5,000 and serves around 500 people with safe drinking water. In Rajasthan, India, the NGO Tarun Bharat Sangh revived traditional water harvesting techniques: johads (earthen check dams that capture rainwater and recharge groundwater) and baoris (traditional step wells). Over 8,000 johads were restored across 850 villages — rivers that had dried up began flowing again. Other sustainable approaches include: rainwater harvesting (collecting roof runoff into tanks — used in India, parts of Africa and UK green buildings); grey water recycling (reusing washing-up/bath water for toilet flushing); drip irrigation (delivers water directly to plant roots — 30–50% more efficient than surface irrigation); fixing leaking pipes; water metering (reduces household use by approximately 15% in UK trials); and education and awareness campaigns.
writeBullets: WaterAid — boreholes with hand pumps; ~£5,000 per borehole; serves ~500 people
Rajasthan johads — earthen check dams; revived rivers; 850 villages benefited
Rajasthan baoris — traditional step wells; groundwater recharge
Rainwater harvesting — roof runoff into tanks; used in India, Africa, UK green buildings
Grey water recycling — bath/washing-up water reused for toilets
Drip irrigation — 30–50% more efficient than surface irrigation; direct to roots
Water metering — reduces household use by ~15% in UK trials
keyPoints.memTip: WaterAid digs wells; Rajasthan revives ancient johads — both work with nature, not against it.
keyPoints.tutor_note: The Rajasthan johad revival is a brilliant example — it shows indigenous knowledge and community-led solutions can be highly effective. Contrast with the top-down China project.

tables.title: Large-scale vs small-scale water management — comparison
rows: Moves large volumes; addresses major regional imbalances
Hugely expensive ($78bn); displaces communities; ecological impacts on donor region
Creates new water from seawater; reliable supply for dry regions
Very energy-intensive; expensive; brine damages marine environments
Saudi Arabia — 33 plants provide 70%+ of drinking water
Reliable water storage; bonus HEP generation
Small scale — only serves ~500 per borehole
Uses local knowledge; recharges groundwater; revived dried rivers
Requires community participation; works best in specific rainfall regimes

1.question: What happened to the Aral Sea and why is it used as an example of water insecurity?
options: Soviet irrigation schemes for cotton farming diverted its feeder rivers, causing it to lose 90% of its volume — turning it from the world's 4th largest lake into toxic salt flats
Industrial pollution from nearby factories contaminated the Aral Sea, making its water undrinkable despite still having high water levels
Climate change reduced rainfall in the region by 90%, causing the Aral Sea to gradually shrink over 200 years
An earthquake caused the Aral Sea's main outlet to open, draining most of its water into underground cave systems
1.correctFeedback: Exactly right. The Aral Sea is one of the world's most dramatic environmental disasters — Soviet irrigation schemes diverted the Amu Darya and Syr Darya rivers to grow cotton, depriving the lake of its water source. It lost 90% of its volume, destroying the fishing industry and exposing toxic salt and pesticide-laden dust.
1.wrongFeedback: Not quite. The Aral Sea disaster was caused by human overuse — Soviet cotton irrigation schemes diverted its feeder rivers, causing it to lose 90% of its volume. It went from the world's 4th largest lake to toxic salt flats — a stark example of what overuse of water causes.

3.question: Why is WaterAid's borehole programme considered a more sustainable approach than China's South-North Water Transfer Project?
options: It is community-managed, low-cost (~£5,000 per borehole), uses appropriate technology and requires no displacement of people
It uses satellite technology to locate aquifers, making it technically superior to China's canal-based approach
It is funded by the Chinese government as part of its international development programme
It transfers water from water-surplus countries to water-scarce countries across international borders
3.correctFeedback: Correct! WaterAid's boreholes are sustainable because they're small-scale, community-managed, low-cost (£5,000 each), require no displacement, and local people are trained to maintain them. The China project cost $78bn and displaced 300,000 people — impressive scale but much less sustainable or equitable.
3.wrongFeedback: Not quite. WaterAid's approach is considered more sustainable because it is low-cost (~£5,000 per borehole), community-managed with local training, and requires no displacement — compared to China's $78bn project that displaced 300,000 people.

commonMistakes: Saying 'water is scarce because there isn't much' — water scarcity is often caused by pollution, overuse, poor infrastructure or uneven distribution, not just low global supply.
Forgetting that agriculture uses 70% of freshwater globally — students often focus on domestic use, but farming is by far the biggest consumer.
Describing desalination as simple and cheap — it is very energy-intensive and expensive; the concentrated brine disposal also causes environmental problems.
Confusing the Aral Sea (overuse — irrigation) with the Ganges (pollution — industrial and sewage waste) — they are both water insecurity examples but with different causes.
Not evaluating large-scale schemes — the China transfer project displaced 300,000 people; Three Gorges displaced 1.3 million. Always mention social costs alongside benefits.
examTips: For 9-mark water management comparison questions: one paragraph on large-scale (with China/Saudi Arabia evidence), one on small-scale/sustainable (WaterAid or Rajasthan), then evaluate which is more appropriate or sustainable.
The Aral Sea is perfect for 'consequences of water overuse' questions — it's dramatic, well-documented, and shows ecological, economic and social consequences simultaneously.
When describing WaterAid, give the cost and scale (£5,000 per borehole, serves 500 people) — these specific figures show you have real knowledge rather than vague ideas.
UK water questions: always mention that SE England is water-stressed (not that the UK as a whole is) — many students incorrectly think high UK rainfall means no water stress.
revisionCardBullets: Agriculture = 70% of global freshwater; industry 20%; domestic 10%
Egypt — 97% of water from the Nile; extreme dependency on one river
Causes: low rainfall, pollution (Ganges), overuse (Aral Sea), poor infrastructure, climate change
Aral Sea — 4th largest lake; 90% volume lost to Soviet cotton irrigation; ecological disaster
Desalination — Saudi Arabia: 33 plants, 70%+ of drinking water; energy-intensive; brine issues
Three Gorges Dam — world's largest; 22.5 GW; displaced 1.3m people
WaterAid boreholes — ~£5,000; serves ~500 people; community-managed; rural Africa and Asia
Rajasthan johads — earthen check dams; revived groundwater; 850 villages benefited
Drip irrigation — 30–50% more efficient than surface irrigation
Water metering — reduces UK household use by ~15%
